findings.title: TransientError.__init__(*args: Any) hides the real call shapes behind untyped variadic branching
findings.body: The consolidated constructor is `def __init__(self, *args: Any) -> None:` with runtime isinstance(args[0], int) and not isinstance(args[0], bool) branching to decide whether this is a bare-status call (TransientError(429)), a status+detail call (TransientError(503, resp.text)), or a plain-message call (TransientError("msg")). *args: Any gives mypy nothing to check: TransientError(1, 2, 3, 4) or TransientError("x", 1) type-check fine and only fail (or silently produce a wrong message) at runtime, since the else branch just forwards all args to Exception.__init__. This is precisely the kind of type-hint-dodging shape the four original per-adapter _TransientHTTPError classes did not have -- each had an explicit, single-shaped __init__(self, status_code: int) (or status, body for llm_anchor_provider's version). Three call shapes bolted onto one signature via positional-arg sniffing is harder to read at call sites (TransientError(503, body) vs TransientError("msg") look identical syntactically) and harder to verify statically than either (a) an explicit multi-parameter constructor, e.g. __init__(self, message: str | None = None, *, status_code: int | None = None, detail: str | None = None), or (b) keeping the plain-message constructor and adding TransientError.from_status(code: int, detail: str | None = None) as a @classmethod factory for the HTTP-status call sites. Either would let every call site self-document its intent and let mypy catch a misuse instead of silently falling into the else branch. The 'not isinstance(args[0], bool)' guard is also speculative cleverness -- no call site anywhere in src/ ever passes a bool, so it defends against a case that cannot occur while adding a subtle 'why does this check for bool' detour for the reader.
findings.recommendation: Replace the *args sniffing with an explicit constructor (message plus optional keyword-only status_code/detail) or a TransientError.from_status(code, detail=None) classmethod factory, and update the 5 call sites (blogger_api.py, medium_api.py x2, velog_graphql.py, llm_anchor_provider.py x2) to use it explicitly.

findings.title: ChromeLaunchError.detail is written but never read anywhere -- the scrubbed diagnostic text this fix introduces is effectively discarded
findings.body: D3 correctly stops arbitrary exception text from being smuggled into ChromeLaunchError.error_code (a closed enum bind_job.py looks up via BIND_ERROR_MESSAGES.get(error_code, ...)), moving the scrubbed text to a new `detail: str | None` attribute instead (chrome_backend.py:70: raise ChromeLaunchError("chrome_not_available", detail=cleaned) from exc). But grepping the whole tree for '.detail' across src/ and webui_app/ turns up only the assignment site and test assertions -- no production code ever reads exc.detail. run_bind's except-clauses in _driver_impl.py only catch PlaywrightLaunchError / BoundPredicateTimeout / IdentityMismatch, never ChromeLaunchError, so a raised ChromeLaunchError propagates out to bind_channel.py's catch-all (except Exception as exc: ... str(exc)), and str(exc) on a RuntimeError built via super().__init__(error_code) returns only the fixed literal error_code, not detail. handle_unexpected_error in _util/errors.py similarly only prints f"unexpected error: {exc}". So the security fix is real, but the diagnostic value it claims to preserve (the commit message: 'the wrapped exception's own (scrubbed) text goes on the separate detail attribute') has no consumer -- an operator debugging a real Chrome-launch failure now sees strictly less information than before (previously-leaky, but visible) with nothing replacing it. This reads like a half-finished plumbing change: the data model is right, but nothing was wired to surface it.
findings.recommendation: Either log exc.detail (e.g. log.warning(..., detail=exc.detail)) at the site where ChromeLaunchError is ultimately handled, or forward it into the channel.bind.failed event under a field the WebUI already ignores for enum purposes (mirroring how _project_reducers.py forwards scrub_hits alongside a cleaned message) -- otherwise drop the detail param and note in the docstring that the diagnostic text is intentionally discarded, so a future reader doesn't assume there's an existing consumer.

findings.title: scrub_text() hit-count is silently discarded at the new bind-failure event-emission sites, unlike the codebase's existing precedent of forwarding it for security observability
findings.body: Both new catch-all sites do cleaned_message, _hits = scrub_text(str(exc)) and only forward cleaned_message into the channel.bind.failed event -- the hit-count (which patterns matched, e.g. oauth_bearer, cookie_header, high_entropy) is thrown away. This is exactly the call site the commit's own rationale flags as security-sensitive ('the actual unfiltered stdout->WebUI exit point for any exception... lands here'), which is precisely where knowing that a secret-shaped string was actually redacted is itself a useful security signal -- if an adapter's exception message routinely contains a credential-shaped token, that's worth alerting on, not just quietly cleaning. The codebase already has this exact pattern the other way: events/_project_reducers.py's two PUBLISH_FAILED call sites capture cleaned, hits = scrub_text(...) and persist "scrub_hits": hits or {} alongside the cleaned message in the event payload. The new D3 sites emit a structurally similar 'failed' event (channel/error_code/message) through the same event substrate but drop the hit-count entirely, which reads as an inconsistency with the established convention rather than a deliberate simplification -- nothing in the commit message explains why this site should behave differently from _project_reducers.py's.
findings.recommendation: Either forward _hits as a scrub_hits field on the channel.bind.failed event (matching _project_reducers.py's PUBLISH_FAILED convention), or note explicitly why bind-failure events don't need the same observability signal publish-failure events get.

residual_risks: chrome_backend.py's RealChromeBrowserRunner (and therefore ChromeLaunchError's raise sites) does not appear to be wired as the default _browser_runner anywhere in production code (cli/admin/bind_channel.py main()'s _browser_runner defaults to None -> _PlaywrightBrowserRunner()); if that's still true, the D3 ChromeLaunchError/detail work is currently only exercised by tests, not by any live code path -- worth confirming this backend is actually reachable before treating its error-handling polish as user-facing.

The '# debt: <slug>' comment convention added across roughly 30 files in D2/D3 is pre-existing and tool-enforced (test_debt_registry_format.py cross-references every comment against debt_registry.toml), so it is a deliberate project convention rather than new clutter -- but the per-file density (multiple debt comments in some files) is worth watching as a readability tax if the sweep continues into other subsystems.
testing_gaps: No unit test exercises TransientError's consolidated dual-shape constructor directly (e.g. TransientError(503, "body").status_code == 503 and the message format for both branches) -- tests/test_adapter_base.py only re-asserts the pre-existing plain-message shape (TransientError("test transient error")); the two-arg and status_code-attribute behavior is only exercised indirectly through adapter-level retry tests.

No test asserts on ChromeLaunchError.detail being read/logged anywhere downstream (only that it's set correctly at the raise site) -- consistent with the finding above that nothing downstream currently consumes it.
